questions.question_text: Under NM's prior appropriation doctrine, who owns all water in the state?
distractors: The property owner where water is located
The federal government
The county where water flows
questions.explanation: In New Mexico, all water belongs to the public (the state). Private parties acquire the right to use water, not ownership of the water itself.

questions.question_text: Under prior appropriation, when two water users compete for the same source, priority goes to:
questions.correct_answer: The user who first put water to beneficial use
distractors: The user whose property is closest to the source
The user with the larger property
The user who filed the most recent permit
questions.explanation: Prior appropriation follows 'first in time, first in right.' The user who first put water to beneficial use has priority, regardless of property location or size.

questions.question_text: Water rights in NM are treated as:
distractors: Automatically included with land ownership
Part of mineral rights
Owned by the county government
questions.explanation: In NM, water rights are separate from land ownership and can be sold, leased, or transferred independently. A land sale does not automatically include water rights.

questions.question_text: Which state office administers water rights and adjudicates disputes in NM?
questions.correct_answer: The Office of the State Engineer
distractors: The NM Environment Department
The NM Real Estate Commission
The county water authority
questions.explanation: The Office of the State Engineer (OSE) administers water rights, issues permits, and adjudicates water disputes in New Mexico.

questions.question_text: A buyer purchases rural land in NM but does not verify water rights. The buyer may discover:
questions.correct_answer: The land has no legal right to use water
distractors: Water rights always transfer with the land
The county will provide water automatically
Federal law guarantees rural water access
questions.explanation: Because water rights are separate from land ownership in NM, a buyer must verify water availability and legal rights. Purchasing land does not guarantee water access.

questions.question_text: In NM, a domestic well for household use can generally be drilled without a permit if usage does not exceed:
distractors: 1 acre-foot per year
5 acre-feet per year
10 acre-feet per year
questions.explanation: Domestic wells for household use up to 3 acre-feet per year can generally be drilled without an OSE permit. Larger groundwater uses require a permit.

questions.question_text: A farmer wants to divert water from a NM river for irrigation. The farmer must first obtain a permit from:
questions.correct_answer: The Office of the State Engineer
distractors: The county water district
The NM Environment Department
The US Army Corps of Engineers
questions.explanation: Surface water rights require a permit from the Office of the State Engineer. All surface water diversions in NM must be permitted through the OSE.

questions.correct_answer: Traditional community irrigation ditches in NM law
distractors: Modern municipal water treatment plant facilities
Federal government water distribution network systems
Private wells shared between two adjoining properties
questions.explanation: Acequias are traditional community irrigation ditch systems with roots in Spanish colonial law. NM law recognizes acequia associations and their authority to govern water distribution among members.

questions.question_text: A property is located within an acequia association's territory. Water distribution among members is governed by:
distractors: The county water department
The Office of the State Engineer directly
Each property owner independently
questions.explanation: Acequia associations govern water distribution among their members. These associations have legal authority under NM law to manage water allocation within their ditch systems.

questions.question_text: A rancher wants to drill a commercial well to irrigate 100 acres. Unlike a domestic well, this use:
distractors: Is exempt if on the rancher's own land
Only needs county approval
Is automatically granted upon application
questions.explanation: Commercial and agricultural water uses exceeding 3 acre-feet per year require a permit from the OSE. The domestic well exemption only covers household use up to 3 acre-feet.

questions.question_text: Which state agency regulates septic systems in NM?
distractors: The NM Real Estate Commission
The county health department only
The Office of the State Engineer
questions.explanation: NMED regulates liquid waste disposal, including septic systems. Permits for installation and modification must comply with NMED regulations.

questions.question_text: A homeowner wants to install a new septic system on their NM property. What must they obtain first?
distractors: Only a building permit from the county
Approval from the HOA board
A variance from the zoning commission
questions.explanation: Septic system installation requires a permit from NMED (New Mexico Environment Department). The system must comply with NMED regulations for setbacks, soil type, and capacity.

questions.question_text: When selling a property with a septic system in NM, the seller should:
questions.correct_answer: Disclose the type and condition of the system
distractors: Replace the system before closing
Provide a 5-year warranty on the system
Obtain a new NMED permit for the buyer
questions.explanation: Sellers should disclose the type and condition of septic systems to buyers. This allows buyers to evaluate the system and plan for any necessary inspections or repairs.

questions.question_text: A septic system permit in NM must ensure compliance with regulations regarding:
questions.correct_answer: Setbacks, soil type, and system capacity
distractors: Only the distance from the property line
Only the number of bedrooms in the house
Only the depth of the water table
questions.explanation: NMED septic regulations address setbacks (distance from wells, property lines, water sources), soil type (percolation ability), and system capacity (based on home size and expected use).

questions.question_text: A buyer is purchasing a rural NM property not connected to municipal sewer. The buyer should be aware that:
questions.correct_answer: The property likely relies on a septic system
distractors: NM law prohibits septic systems on rural land
The county provides free sewer connections
Septic systems are not regulated in rural areas
questions.explanation: Properties not connected to municipal sewer systems require septic systems in NM. NMED regulates these systems, and buyers should verify the system's compliance and condition.
